summary: Gruber enthusiastically recommends Apple TV+'s new series 'The Studio,' starring Seth Rogen, calling it fantastic after its first two episodes. He draws comparisons to Robert Altman's 'The Player,' noting both works are themselves intricate cinematic art about the struggle to create art within corporate Hollywood. He uses the show as a springboard to theorize that Apple's motivation for original content is a genuine belief that great cinema needs corporate champions willing to invest in art. He argues Apple TV+ has the highest hit rate for quality shows among all streamers, even if their movies lag behind.
key_points: The Studio is a meta-cinematic achievement that embodies the very artistic ambition its characters fear is dying in corporate Hollywood
The show's biggest inspiration is Robert Altman's The Player, which itself paid homage to Orson Welles's Touch of Evil, creating a lineage of self-aware cinema about cinema
Apple's motivation for TV+ may simply be a sincere belief that great cinema needs deep-pocketed champions, and Apple sees it as an obligation to fill that role
Apple TV+ has the highest hit rate for quality shows among all streamers, though their movie output has been largely disappointing
The decline of other prestige outlets like HBO (now subsumed by Max) has created an opening that Apple is uniquely filling
money_quotes: They *are* what they're *about*. They achieve what the characters within them fear is no longer achievable.
Perhaps Apple's leadership simply believes, as I do, that cinema is the grandest and greatest form of art the world has ever seen.
Great cinema is expensive and delicate and needs, from deep-pocketed studios and their deeper-pocketed corporate parents, more than patrons, but *champions*.
In a media landscape where such champions of cinema-as-art and art-as-an-essential-public-good are fewer and fewer, it is Apple's not just opportunity but *obligation* to step up to the plate.
I've long thought that one of the minor tragedies of Steve Jobs's second act is that the timing just didn't work out to sell Pixar to Apple, instead of to Disney.
No streamer has a higher hit rate for quality shows.
Ben Stiller delivered a season 2 finale that's arguably the best 78 minutes of filmmaking I've seen this decade.
Netflix almost never makes shows like this. Neither does HBO now that's its been subsumed by 'Max'. Peacock et al don't even try.
key_insight: The Studio exemplifies why Apple TV+ exists: Apple sees itself as an obligated champion of cinematic art in an era when other studios have abandoned that mission.
